intro:
Redemption is deliverance from bondage by the payment of a price. The Hebrew pādâ ("to ransom") and gāʼal ("to act as kinsman-redeemer"), and the Greek agorazō ("to buy in the marketplace") and lytroō / apolytrōsis ("release by ransom"), all picture the same reality: a captive set free because a redeemer pays what is required.

The Old Testament’s paradigm is the Exodus: "I will redeem you with an outstretched arm" (Exodus 6:6), God buying Israel out of slavery (Deuteronomy 7:8). The law provided a kinsman-redeemer who could buy back a relative’s person or land (Leviticus 25:47–49) — embodied by Boaz in Ruth — and Job confessed, "I know that my Redeemer lives" (Job 19:25). The LORD himself is repeatedly called Israel’s Redeemer (Isaiah 44:6).

Christ is the true Redeemer, and the price is his own blood: "in him we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of our trespasses" (Ephesians 1:7; Colossians 1:14). He came "to give his life as a ransom for many" (Mark 10:45; 1 Timothy 2:6); believers were "bought with a price" (1 Corinthians 6:20), "ransomed… not with perishable things… but with the precious blood of Christ" (1 Peter 1:18–19), and redeemed "from the curse of the law" (Galatians 3:13).

Redemption’s scope is total — from sin’s penalty (forgiveness), its power (a people "purified… zealous for good works," Titus 2:14), and finally its presence: "the redemption of our bodies" at the resurrection (Romans 8:23; Ephesians 1:14) and the renewal of all things. So the redeemed sing that the Lamb "by your blood… ransomed people for God from every tribe and language" (Revelation 5:9): God himself pays to make us his own.
